4: Goliath is introduced as ʾîš habênayim — 'man of the between-two-spaces' — the technical term for a single combat champion who stands between the two armies to settle the battle by representative duel. The bênayim (the space between) is the no-man's land where the champions meet; the champion's defeat ends the battle. This is the ANE institution of monomachia — individual combat as proxy warfare. The irony of the narrative is that Israel has a champion — YHWH — who dwells in the bênayim between heaven and earth, whose representative David will enter the space as YHWH's proxy. Goliath is the embodiment of all that looks like an unsurmountable adversary: six cubits and a span tall, armored in bronze, with a spear like a weaver's beam. The narrative establishes the appearance of insurmountability before the reversal.
26: David's first question — mî haʿārēl happəlištî hazzeh kî ḥērēp maʿarəḵôt ʾelōhîm ḥayyîm — 'Who is this uncircumcised Philistine that he should defy the armies of the living God?' — introduces the key theological term ḥerpāh (reproach, defiance, shame). Goliath's challenge is not merely military; it is a covenant-theological defiance: he stands against ʾelōhîm ḥayyîm (the living God), which David will address directly in v45-46. The term ḥerpāh (shame/reproach) appears in Psalms 22:6 — 'I am a reproach (ḥerpāh) to men' — where the Davidic king takes on the shame that Goliath levied. The one who defeats the shame-levying giant in 1 Samuel 17 will himself bear shame in Ps 22 — the trajectory from victorious champion to suffering servant in the Davidic typology.
45: David's declaration of battle terms is a liturgical counter to Goliath's military credentials: ʾattāh bāʾ ʾēlay bəḥereḇ ûḇaḥănît ûḇəḵîḏôn wəʾānōḵî bāʾ ʾēleyḵā bəšēm YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt — 'You come to me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come to you in the name of YHWH of armies.' The contrast is between weaponry (ḥereḇ, ḥănît, kîḏôn = sword, spear, javelin — three weapons for Goliath's three-fold armament) and the šēm YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt (the name of YHWH of armies). The name (šēm) is the representative presence of YHWH — invoked in battle as the living divine warrior (cf. Psalms 20:5 — 'in the name of our God we will set up our banners'). YHWH ṣəḇāʾôt (YHWH of armies) is the divine warrior title par excellence; David is fighting as YHWH's champion, and the šēm is his weapon.
47: David's theological summary of the battle: wəyēḏᵉʿû kol haqqāhāl hazzeh kî lōʾ bəḥereḇ ûḇaḥănît yôšîaʿ YHWH kî laYHWH hammilḥāmāh — 'all this assembly will know that not by sword or spear does YHWH save; for the battle belongs to YHWH.' The phrase laYHWH hammilḥāmāh (to YHWH belongs the battle) is the most compressed statement of holy war theology in the Samuel narratives. It echoes Moses at the Red Sea (Exodus 14:13-14 — 'Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH... YHWH will fight for you') and Jehoshaphat's battle prayer (2 Chronicles 20:15 — 'the battle is not yours but God's'). The purpose of the victory is explicitly missional: 'that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel' (v46) — YHWH's glory among the nations as the goal of the anointed's battle.
49: The sling-stone strikes Goliath in the forehead (miṣḥô): wayyitbāʿ hāʾeḇen bᵉmiṣḥô wayyipōl ʿal pānāyw ʾārṣāh — 'the stone sank into his forehead, and he fell on his face to the ground.' The blow to the head is the Genesis 3:15 vocabulary enacted: rōʾš (head) — 'he shall crush your head' (yəšûpəḵā rōʾš). The serpent's-head-crushing prophecy finds one of its clearest pre-Christ actualizations here: the seed of the woman (the young shepherd, the least expected champion) strikes the representative of all forces arrayed against YHWH's people through the head. The forehead strike is not incidental but structurally significant — it is the narrative's way of gesturing toward Genesis 3:15 while pointing beyond all human champion-combatants to the one who will finally crush the serpent.
51: David takes Goliath's own sword and cuts off his head: wayyiqaḥ dāwiḏ ʾet ḥarbô wayyišlᵉpāh mittaʿrāh wayyᵉmōtəṯēhû wayyiḵrat bāh ʾet rōʾšô — 'David took his sword and drew it from its sheath and killed him and cut off his head with it.' The use of the enemy's own weapon to execute the final blow is the book's ironic statement: the armament that was supposed to terrify Israel becomes the instrument of Goliath's destruction. This is the NT pattern for Christ's victory over death: 'that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death' (Hebrews 2:14) — the weapon of the adversary (death) becomes the mechanism of the adversary's defeat. David takes the giant's head to Jerusalem (v54), presaging the future capital where the Davidic line will reign and ultimately where the serpent's head will be crushed once for all.
1: wayyiqbəṣû pəlištîm ʾeṯ-maḥănêhem lammiḥlāmāh — 'gathered their camps/forces for battle.' The term maḥăneh (camp, army) is both the military encampment and the assembled force within it; the MKT 'forces' captures the military sense. Socoh (Khirbet Abbad, c.21km WSW of Bethlehem) is in the Shephelah foothills — the contested buffer zone between Philistia and Judah. The Philistines are staging in Israelite territory, forcing Israel to respond defensively.
2: ʾēmeq hāʾēlāh — 'Valley of the Terebinth' (MKT: 'Valley of Elah'). The name derives from the ʾēlāh tree (terebinth or oak). The site is identified with Wadi es-Sant, where a dry streambed provided the smooth stones David would select (v40). The two armies deploy on opposite hills with the valley between — a standard ANE tactical standoff waiting for an individual champion or breakthrough.
3: The spatial geometry — Philistines on one hill, Israel on another, haggayʾ bênêhem ('the valley between them') — establishes the impasse that Goliath's challenge is designed to break. In ANE warfare, champion combat was an accepted device for avoiding mass casualties; the text presents this as a genuine Philistine proposal, not mere bravado. The valley functions as a no-man's-land until David crosses it (v48).
5: qoḇaʿ nəḥōšeṯ ʿal-rōʾšô wəširiôn qaśqaśîm hûʾ lāḇûš — bronze helmet and scale armor. The širiôn qaśqaśîm ('coat of scale armor,' lit. 'armor of scales') is the laminar bronze plate-suit documented in ANE archaeology and known from Ugaritic and Egyptian texts; the 5,000-shekel weight (~57kg) is plausible for full body-armor. The catalogue of Goliath's equipment (vv5–7) follows the literary convention of ekphrasis — detailed description establishing the champion's invincibility before the reversal.
6: kîdôn nəḥōšeṯ bên kētēpāyw — 'a javelin of bronze between his shoulders.' The term kîdôn appears elsewhere as a short lance or dart (Joshua 8:18; Job 39:23); its placement 'between his shoulders' (slung on the back) indicates a third projectile weapon in addition to the spear and sword. Goliath is equipped for every combat range: javelin for distance, spear for mid-range, sword for close combat. The asymmetry with David's sling (v40) is the narrative's central irony.
7: wəḥēṣ ḥaniṯô kəmənôr ʾōrəgîm — 'the shaft of his spear was like a weaver's beam.' The comparison to a weaver's beam (the horizontal roller on a loom, typically 5–7cm thick) communicates mass rather than size. The iron point weighing 600 shekels (~6.8kg) represents high-grade iron weaponry; the Philistines had metallurgical superiority in iron at this period (1 Samuel 13:19–22). The shield-bearer walking ahead completes the picture of a fully-serviced war machine.
8: ʿibrîm ʾattem ʿăḇādîm ləšāʾûl — 'you are Hebrews, servants of Saul.' Goliath's use of ʿibrîm ('Hebrews') rather than 'Israel' is the outsider's ethnic label, associated in Samuel with Philistine usage and carrying social-inferior connotations (cf. 1 Samuel 4:6, 9; 1 Samuel 13:3, 7, 19). The legal framework of the challenge — loser's side becomes servants (ʿăḇādîm) of the winner — formalises the combat as a binding covenant-substitution.
9: The conditional structure of the challenge — 'if he can kill me, we serve you; if I kill him, you serve us' — is legally precise. The verb wəʿăḇaḏtem ʾōṯānû ('you shall serve us') carries the full weight of tributary servitude. The Philistine champions's challenge inverts the covenant structure: Israel's God should give them victory; instead they must produce a man who can match Goliath. The narrative will resolve by collapsing the human champion structure through YHWH's intervention via David.
10: ḥēraptî ʾeṯ-maʿarəḵôṯ yiśrāʾēl — 'I have defied/taunted the battle lines of Israel.' The verb ḥārap (Piel: to taunt, revile, reproach) recurs as the chapter's theological key: Goliath is not merely challenging individual combat but insulting maʿarəḵôṯ ʾĕlōhîm ḥayyîm (v26, 'the battle lines of the living God'). David will reframe Goliath's challenge as an insult against YHWH personally (vv45–46), transforming individual combat into a theological contest.
11: wayyišməʿ šāʾûl wəkol-yiśrāʾēl ʾeṯ-dib·rê happəlištî hāʾēlleh wayyēḥattû wayyirəʾû məʾōḏ — they were 'dismayed' (ḥāṯaṯ) and 'greatly afraid.' ḥāṯaṯ is the technical term for the psychological collapse of military courage (Deuteronomy 1:21; Joshua 1:9; Isaiah 8:9); it is the fear that undoes battle readiness. Saul himself, anointed king to lead Israel's armies (1 Samuel 8:20), is paralysed alongside his troops — an indictment of the existing leadership framework.
12: The narrator introduces David's genealogy at the moment of Israel's crisis — a narrative device signalling that the solution to the impasse has already been prepared. ʾepraṯî mibbêṯ leḥem yəhûḏāh ('an Ephrathite from Bethlehem of Judah') is the full ethnic-geographical identification; ʾepraṯî designates the Bethlehemite clan (Ruth 1:2; Micah 5:2). Jesse's advanced age and eight sons establishes the family context that will make David's appearance at the front unexpected.
13: The three eldest sons — Eliab, Abinadab, Shammah — are named and identified with Saul's army. Their presence at the front and David's absence sets up the contrast: David is not a soldier but a shepherd, which is precisely the background that qualifies him for what follows (v34). The naming of the three eldest also establishes Eliab as the eldest who had seemed to Samuel the obvious choice (1 Samuel 16:6–7) but was rejected. His reappearance in v28 will show his character.
14: wədāwid hûʾ haqqāṭōn — 'David was the youngest (qāṭōn).' The adjective qāṭōn (small, youngest, least significant) is precisely the category YHWH overturns in 1 Samuel's theology (1 Samuel 16:7, 11; cf. 1 Samuel 9:21 — Saul's own initial self-deprecation). The youngest son sent to provision his brothers recapitulates the Joseph pattern (Genesis 37:13–14) and points toward the reversal the narrative is constructing.
15: wədāwid hōlēk wāšāb mēʿal šāʾûl — 'David went back and forth from Saul to tend his father's flock.' The verb hōlēk wāšāb (going and returning) describes a commuter pattern; David has a dual role as court musician (1 Samuel 16:18–23) and pastoral shepherd. This verse is sometimes cited in the textual discussion about the apparent narrative tension between 1 Samuel 16 and 1 Samuel 17; the MKT renders it naturally as a continuing pattern of alternating service.
16: wayyaškem happəlištî wayyaʿărōḇ baqqer ûḇāʿereḇ — the Philistine 'rose early and presented himself morning and evening for forty days.' The twice-daily challenge ('morning and evening') mirrors the twice-daily tamid offering in the tabernacle (Ex 1 Samuel 29:38–42) — a dark parody of liturgical regularity. Forty days is the standard biblical period of testing (Numbers 13:25; Jonah 3:4; Matthew 4:2). The challenge has liturgical rhythm but no covenantal content.
17: Jesse's errand — ʾēpāh qālûy ʾeśer leḥem ('an ephah of roasted grain and ten loaves') — is a practical supply run, not a heroic mission. The provisions are standard field rations: roasted grain (qālî) was portable, shelf-stable army food (Ruth 2:14; 2 Samuel 17:28). Ten cuts of cheese (ḥaṣar ḥĕlāḇîm) go to the unit commander — small gifts of honour. Jesse's errand positions David at the battle line without any military mandate, making his intervention entirely contingent.
18: wəʾeṯ-ʾaḥêkā tifqōḏ ləšālôm wəʾeṯ-ʿarūbbāṯām tiqkāḥ — 'check on your brothers' welfare (šālôm) and bring back some token from them.' The word ʿǎrubbāh (pledge, token — given as security) is unusual here; some read it as 'word/news' (emending slightly). The MKT 'token' captures the sense of tangible evidence that David reached the brothers safely. Jesse's concern is fraternal welfare, not military intelligence.
19: The narrative locates Jesse's sons and 'all the men of Israel' in the Valley of Elah 'fighting (nilḥāmîm) the Philistines.' The participle nilḥāmîm is technically a stalemate — they are 'engaged in battle' without resolution. The passive standoff (armies drawn up but no engagement, v21) is where David arrives. The narrator's vocabulary holds two realities: formally there is a war, practically there is a 40-day paralysis.
20: wayyaškēm dāwid babbōqer wayyaʿazeḇ ʾeṯ-haṣṣōʾn ʿal yad šômēr — David 'rose early in the morning and left the flock with a keeper.' Early rising (wayyaškēm) is the mark of diligence throughout the Samuel narrative; leaving the sheep properly attended rather than abandoned shows pastoral responsibility. His arrival at the camp happens to coincide with the battle array drawing up (v21) — the narrator frames the coincidence as providential.
21: wayyaʿarəḵû yiśrāʾēl ûpəlištîm maʿarāḵāh liḳrāṯ maʿarāḵāh — 'Israel and the Philistines drew up battle formation facing each other.' The repetition of maʿarāḵāh ('battle formation, array, line') frames the scene as pure standoff: two battle lines facing each other with no one willing to move. David arrives precisely at the moment of maximum tension and minimum action — when the impasse is most visible and most humiliating.
22: wayyaʿazeḇ dāwid ʾeṯ-kēlāyw ʿal yad šômēr hakkēlîm — 'David left his gear with the keeper of equipment.' The transition from errand-boy to soldier is marked by this administrative detail: David formally checks his supplies with the supply officer before running to the battle line to greet his brothers. The mundane logistics grounds the heroic narrative.
23: Goliath's appearance and repetition of his challenge (kaddəḇārîm hāʾēlleh, 'those same words') is the narrative trigger. David hears the challenge for the first time at the same moment Goliath delivers it; the repetition for the reader (hearing it the second time) is David's first encounter. His response will be immediate and theologically formed — unlike the forty-day paralysis of the army.
24: wayyānusû mippānāyw wayyirəʾû məʾōḏ — 'they fled from before him and were very afraid.' The verb nûs (to flee, to run away in fear) describes actual retreat from Goliath's advance, not merely standing their ground in fear. This amplifies the impasse: Goliath can actually advance and make Israel run, yet the formal battle has not begun. The daily intimidation has become a rehearsal of defeat.
25: wəhāyāh hāʾîš ʾăšer yakkennû yaʿăśernû hammelek ʿāšer gādôl — 'the king will enrich the man who kills him greatly.' The reward structure — wealth (ʿāšer), the king's daughter, and freedom for his father's house from tax/tribute — is the ANE hero's reward. The detail of freedom from ḥopšî ('freed household,' possibly exemption from corvée or tribute) adds legal specificity. David's question about this reward in v26 shows he is listening to the political economy, not just the military challenge.
27: The men repeat the reward terms to David: kazzeh yaʿăśeh lāʾîš ʾăšer yakkennû ('this is what will be done for the man who kills him'). The passive construction ('will be done') implies official royal promise; David's inquiry about it in v26 and its repetition here establishes the formal covenantal framework of the reward before David acts. He is not acting from naivety but from full knowledge of the terms.
28: wayyiḥar ʾap-ʾĕlîāḇ bədāwid — 'Eliab's anger burned against David.' ḥārāh ʾap (lit. 'the nose burned') is the standard Hebrew idiom for fierce anger. Eliab's accusation — pəḥazûṯ libbəkā ('the presumption/insolence of your heart') — is exactly the false reading: the reader knows David's heart has been examined by YHWH (1 Samuel 16:7) and found worthy. The eldest brother's dismissal is the final repetition of the 16:7 theme — human sight judges wrongly.
29: māh ʿāśîṯî ʿattāh halôʾ dāḇār hûʾ — 'What have I done now? Is it not just talk/a word?' David's response is understated and rhetorical. dāḇār here means a matter worth inquiring about; the question deflects Eliab's attack without engaging it. The brevity is telling: David doesn't defend himself but moves on (v30). The contrast with Eliab's heated accusation underlines David's equanimity.
30: wayyissōḇ mēʾeṣlô ʾel-mul ʾaḥēr — 'he turned away from him to another.' The verb sāḇaḇ (to turn, go around) is physical: David simply walks away from Eliab to ask someone else. The same question produces the same answer; the repetition confirms the reward is publicly known. David's persistence is not stubbornness but methodical information-gathering before action.
31: wayiggāḏû liḵənê šāʾûl — 'what David had said was reported to Saul.' The passive report (wayyaggēḏ, Hiphil, 'was told') shifts the narrative momentum: David's words have now reached the king. Saul's summons (wayyišlaḥ lô, 'he sent for him') sets up the confrontation that will expose Saul's paralysis and provide the opportunity for David's intervention. The passive construction distances David from any self-promotion.
32: ʾal-yippōl lēḇ-ʾādām ʿālāyw — 'let no man's heart fall/fail because of him.' nāpal lēḇ ('heart falls') is the idiom for loss of courage; David is directly addressing the condition described in v11 (ḥāṯaṯ). His self-identification as 'your servant' (ʿaḇdəḵā) is the appropriate court register; his offer to fight the Philistine frames the action as royal service, not personal glory.
33: lōʾ ṯûḵal lāleḵeṯ — 'you are not able to go.' Saul's objection — nəʿar ʾattāh ('you are a young man/boy') and Goliath is a warrior since his youth — names the obvious disproportion. The term nəʿar (youth, boy, servant) can mean anything from infant to military aide; here it emphasises inexperience. Saul's reason is accurate from a purely human perspective; David's response (vv34–37) will reframe the evidence base.
34: ḥōrēh ʾet-ṣōʾn ʾāḇîw — 'tending his father's flock.' David begins his argument with pastoral biography, not military credentials. The encounter with lion and bear (ʾaryēh wəhaḏḏōḇ) establishes a track record of individual combat against superior force — precisely the scenario Goliath presents. The key word is wəhiṣṣaḷtî (Hiphil of nāṣal, 'I rescued'), which David will use again of YHWH in v37.
35: wəhiḵîṯîhû wəhiṣṣaḷtî miqqî — 'I struck it and rescued [the lamb] from its mouth.' The verbs (nākāh, to strike; nāṣal, to rescue) form David's combat vocabulary: he attacks the predator and recovers what was taken. ûḇəqûmô ʿālay wəheḥezaqtî bizeʿqānô ('when it rose against me, I seized it by its beard') describes hand-to-hand wrestling with a lion — the beard of a lion is not a natural fighting target but demonstrates extreme close-range mastery.
36: wəhāyāh hapaləšṯî hāʿārēl hazzeh kəʾaḥaḏ mēhem — 'this uncircumcised Philistine will be like one of them.' David's use of ʿārēl ('uncircumcised') to describe Goliath reframes the combat in covenant terms: circumcision is the sign of the covenant community (Genesis 17:11); an uncircumcised man is outside the covenant, therefore outside YHWH's protection. The same word recurs in v26 — David uses it consistently, not as an insult but as a theological categorisation.
37: yhwh ʾăšer hiṣṣilani — 'YHWH who rescued me.' David's conclusion draws the explicit theological inference from his biographical evidence: the rescuing agent in the lion-bear encounters was YHWH, not his own skill. yaṣṣilēnî miqqāṯ hapəlištî hazzeh — 'he will rescue me from the hand of this Philistine.' The same verb (nāṣal) links past pastoral rescue to anticipated military rescue; Saul's response ('Go! YHWH be with you') is a blessing that implies his acceptance of David's theological case.
38: wayyalbiš šāʾûl ʾeṯ-dāwid maḏ·dāyw — 'Saul dressed David in his own military garments.' The attempt to armour David in Saul's equipment is symbolically charged: Saul is trying to make David a smaller version of himself, a warrior equipped in the same military framework that has failed. qôḇaʿ nəḥōšeṯ ʿal-rōʾšô ('a bronze helmet on his head') echoes Goliath's armour description (v5) — David is being made into a miniature Goliath.
39: waylōʾ yāḵōl lahalōḵ kî lōʾ nissāh — 'he could not walk, for he had not tested them.' The verb nāsāh (to test, try, prove) is the same root used for YHWH testing Abraham (Genesis 22:1) and Israel's wilderness testing. David had not been tested in this military framework — it is not his. His refusal of the armour and return to his shepherd's staff and sling is the rejection of institutional warfare in favour of the only combat mode he has actually proved.
40: wayyiqkāḥ maqqəlô bəyāḏô wayyiḇḥar-lô ḥămîššāh ʾaḇnê-ḥālaqqôṯ min-hannaḥal — 'five smooth stones from the brook.' The detail of five stones (for one shot) has generated much comment; the LXX reads the same. The practical reason is probably the insurance of multiple attempts; the theological point is that one was sufficient (v49). The shepherd's pouch (yalqûṭ hārōʿîm) and sling (qelaʿ) are tools of his real vocation — the ones YHWH has already validated in the field.
41: wōlēḵ ûmaggēn lōʾ lippānāyw — Goliath coming forward 'with his shield-bearer walking before him.' The shield-bearer's function is battlefield protection from missiles — exactly what David intends to use. The shield-bearer walks lippānāyw ('before him'), unwittingly positioning himself to screen Goliath from the front while David approaches. The sling projectile from a distance would not be screened by a man-held shield in any case.
42: wayyibbet happəlištî wayyirʾeh ʾeṯ-dāwid wayyibzēhû — 'looked... and despised him.' The verb bāzāh (to despise, hold in contempt) is significant: Goliath's evaluation of David follows the same logic Eliab used — looking at outward appearance. naʿar ('youth'), ʾaḏmōnî ('ruddy'), yəpēh marʾeh ('handsome') are the visible markers of inexperience. YHWH had looked at the same outward description in 16:12 and chosen differently.
43: hakeleḇ ʾānōḵî kî-ʾattāh bāʾ ʾēlay bammaqqəlôṯ — 'Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks?' The dog-insult is classic ANE combat rhetoric; a keleḇ ('dog') is a contemptible, scavenging animal. Goliath reads the shepherd's staff (maqqēl) as a stick weapon appropriate for a dog-fight, not honourable combat. He curses David 'by his gods' (ʾĕlōhāyw) — invoking Philistine divine authority. The curse by Philistine gods will be answered by YHWH's name (v45).
44: ləḵāh ʾēlay wəʾetənāh ʾeṯ-bəśārəḵā — 'come to me, and I will give your flesh.' Goliath's counter-threat is the mirror of the battlefield consecration formula: the loser's flesh is given to scavenging birds and beasts (ʿôp haššāmayim ûlḇehemōṯ haśśāḏeh). David will use the same language about Goliath and the Philistine army in v46 — the verbal duel matches the physical one, with David turning Goliath's threat back on him.
46: wənāṯattî ʾeṯ-pigrê maḥănēh pəlištîm hayyôm hazzeh — 'I will give the corpses of the Philistine camp this day.' David's proclamation expands Goliath's individual-combat threat into a battle declaration: the entire army will fall. The purpose clause — wəyēḏəʿû kol-hāʾāreṣ kî yēš ʾĕlōhîm ləyiśrāʾēl ('that all the earth may know that there is a God in Israel') — gives the battle cosmic-apologetic significance. Victory will be theological demonstration, not military achievement.
48: wayyāqom dāwid wayyāroṣ hammaʿarāḵāh liḳrāṯ happəlištî — 'David ran quickly toward the battle line to meet the Philistine.' The verb rûṣ (to run) is notable: David initiates the movement, running toward Goliath rather than waiting. This is the opposite of Israel's reaction in v24 (nûs, to flee). The contrast in verbs of movement — flee vs. run toward — is the chapter's climactic physical action before the sling-shot.
50: wayyeḥezaq dāwid min-happəlištî baqqelaʿ ûḇāʾeḇen — 'David prevailed over the Philistine with sling and stone.' The narrator's summary deliberately notes that David 'struck the Philistine and killed him, and there was no sword in David's hand.' The absence of a sword is the point of the whole sequence: conventional weaponry failed; YHWH's intervention through the weakest instrument succeeded. The sword David uses in v51 is Goliath's own.
52: wayyāqumû ʾanšê yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh wayyārîʿû — 'the men of Israel and Judah rose up with a shout.' The explicit distinction 'Israel and Judah' (yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh) is unusual at this period and may reflect the narrator's knowledge of the later division, or a genuine sense of the tribal tension that will surface in Saul's reign. The pursuit from the valley to Gath and Ekron traces the route back into Philistine heartland.
53: wayyāšuḇû ḇənê yiśrāʾēl middālēq ʾaḥărê pəlištîm — 'returned from pursuing the Philistines.' The plundering of the Philistine camp (wayyāḇōzzû ʾeṯ-maḥănêhem) follows the pattern of holy-war aftermath; the victor takes the spoil of the fleeing enemy. The military reversal is complete: Israel, paralysed for forty days, now pursues the Philistines to their own cities.
54: wayyiqaḥ dāwid ʾeṯ-rōʾš happəlištî wayyəḇîʾēhû yərûšālaim — 'David took the Philistine's head and brought it to Jerusalem.' Jerusalem at this time was still Jebusite (cf. v11's note that it was not Israelite); the placement of Goliath's head there anticipates David's future conquest of the city. The armour is deposited in David's own tent (ʾōhălô), not a royal treasury — David is still a private person at this point.
55: wayōʾmer ʾel-ʾaḇnēr śar haṣṣāḇāʾ ben-mî-zeh hannāʿar — 'Whose son is this young man?' Saul's apparent failure to recognise David (cf. 1 Samuel 16:14–23) has generated extensive scholarly discussion. The most plausible readings are: (a) the narrative derives from independent traditions; (b) Saul genuinely does not know David's lineage (family name), though he knows the person; (c) the question is formulaic for establishing David's family claim to the king's daughter and tax exemption promised in v25.
56: biqqəšû ʾattāh ben-mî-zeh hāʿālem — 'find out whose son this young man is.' ʿālem is a hapax in this form; related to ʿelem (young man of fighting age, cf. 1 Samuel 20:22); the MKT 'young man' is apt. The inquiry is delegated to Abner and then resolved by David's own identification (v58). The genealogical question matters legally: the promised rewards require knowing the family lineage.
57: wayyiqkāḥ ʾōṯô ʾaḇnēr wayyəḇîʾēhû lippənê šāʾûl ûrōʾš happəlištî bəyāḏô — 'Abner brought him before Saul with the Philistine's head still in his hand.' The detail of David holding Goliath's head during his interview with the king is a powerful image of accomplished fact: the promise has been fulfilled, the evidence is literal and immediate. The scene closes the combat narrative and opens the court narrative.
58: ʾanōḵî ben-ʿaḇdəḵā yišay bêṯ hallaḥmî — 'I am the son of your servant Jesse the Bethlehemite.' David's self-identification uses the deferential 'your servant' (ʿaḇdəḵā) for his father, placing himself within the household of a royal servant. The patronymic 'son of Jesse' (ben-yiśay) becomes David's standard identification throughout Samuel and will carry prophetic weight in later passages (cf. Isaiah 11:1, the 'shoot from the stump of Jesse').
